aliases: Rashbi (acronym of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai)

roles: Tanna (rabbinic sage of the Mishnaic period)
traditionally venerated as a mystic and, in the traditional ascription, transmitter of the Zohar
summary: Shimon bar Yochai (Rashbi, second century CE) was a leading Tanna of Roman Palestine, a disciple of Rabbi Akiva, in whose name many teachings are recorded in the Mishnah and Talmud, and who is famous in the Talmud (Shabbat 33b) for hiding in a cave for thirteen years from the Romans. In the later traditional ascription he is venerated as a great mystic and named as the author or transmitter of the Zohar; this ascription is disputed, since academic scholarship since Gershom Scholem attributes the bulk of the Zohar to Moses de Leon in late-thirteenth-century Castile, and both positions are recorded here as attributed without adjudication.
description:
Shimon bar Yochai, known in the rabbinic tradition by the acronym Rashbi (Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai), was a Tanna of the second century CE, one of the principal rabbinic sages of Roman Palestine in the generation after the Bar Kokhba revolt. He is remembered in the rabbinic sources as a leading disciple of Rabbi Akiva, and a very large body of legal and exegetical tradition is transmitted in his name across the Mishnah, the Tosefta, and the halakhic midrashim (the Mekhilta and Sifre are associated with his school), as well as in the Talmud. His teachings are preserved in the open primary corpus: in tractate Avot, for instance, a saying is recorded in his name that 'there are three crowns: the crown of Torah, the crown of priesthood, and the crown of royalty, but the crown of a good name supersedes them all' (Pirkei Avot 4:13).

The best-known narrative about him is the account in the Babylonian Talmud (Shabbat 33b) of his flight from the Roman authorities. In that story he speaks critically of Roman public works ('everything that they established, they established only for their own purposes'), the words are reported, and the sentence is passed that 'Shimon, who denounced the government, shall be killed'; he and his son Rabbi Elazar hide in a cave, where 'a miracle occurred and a carob tree was created for them as well as a spring of water', and they sit buried in sand studying Torah for twelve years, and after a further twelve months emerge, the decree having been abrogated at the death of the emperor. This cave narrative, which survives in an earlier Palestinian version (Yerushalmi Shevi'it 9:1) and a developed Babylonian one, has been the object of close literary and cultural study in the modern scholarship as a rabbinic story rather than as documentary biography.

In the later reception of the tradition, and especially from the medieval period onward, Rashbi came to be venerated as a great mystic, and it is under his name that the Zohar, the central text of Kabbalah, is set: the Zohar's literary frame is a fellowship of sages gathered around Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai in the Galilee expounding the secrets of Torah. It is important to distinguish two things that are attributed to him and to keep them apart. The historical Tanna is attested in the open rabbinic corpus (the Mishnah, the Tosefta, the halakhic midrashim, and the Talmud), where his teachings and the cave narrative are recorded. The ascription of the Zohar to him is a distinct, later, and disputed matter: in the traditional ascription the Zohar preserves his second-century mystical teachings, transmitted and only later written down, while in the modern academic attribution, argued by Gershom Scholem and broadly accepted since, the bulk of the Zohar was composed by the kabbalist Moses de Leon in late-thirteenth-century Castile (c. 1280s) and circulated under Rashbi's name. This entry records the historical Tanna on the tradition's own terms from the open primary sources, and records the Zohar-authorship claim as two attributed positions (the traditional ascription to Rashbi and the academic attribution to de Leon) without ruling between them, exactly as the zohar entry does. Rashbi's traditional day of death (yahrzeit) is observed as a joyous hillula at his tomb at Meron in the Galilee on Lag ba-Omer, a custom given its classic form among the sixteenth-century Safed kabbalists; this is recorded as a feature of the tradition's veneration of him.
editorial_notes: This entry deliberately separates two things: (1) the historical second-century Tanna Shimon bar Yochai, who is attested in the open rabbinic primary corpus and is verified-eligible, and (2) the later traditional mystical ascription of the Zohar to him, which is disputed and handled per neutrality #1-#2 as an attributed position, not adjudicated. The load-bearing content for the historical figure was READ verbatim on open Sefaria and rated high: the cave narrative in the Babylonian Talmud (Shabbat 33b), read at the passage level and quoted (the criticism of Rome, the sentence, the carob tree and spring, the twelve years plus twelve months in the cave, the abrogation of the decree), and a teaching in his name in Pirkei Avot 4:13 (the 'three crowns' saying), also read and quoted. The Zohar-authorship dispute is mirrored on the zohar entry exactly: the traditional ascription (to the second-century Rashbi and his circle) and the modern academic attribution (to Moses de Leon in late-thirteenth-century Castile, c. 1280s, argued by Scholem and broadly accepted since) are BOTH given as attributed positions, the question is stated to be disputed, and the editor does NOT adjudicate. The cave story is presented as a rabbinic narrative studied as such in the scholarship (Rubenstein; Fonrobert), not as documentary biography; the Meron hillula on Lag ba-Omer is recorded as a feature of the tradition's veneration, its classic form dated to the sixteenth-century Safed kabbalists per the scholarship. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-9: (a) academic historians of the aggadah and of Jewish mysticism (Rubenstein, Talmudic Stories, on the Shabbat 33b cave narrative; Fonrobert in the AJS Review on the same passage; Scholem for the disputed Zohar attribution); (b) the open primary texts on Sefaria (the Talmud, Shabbat 33b, and Pirkei Avot 4:13), READ at the passage level and quoted verbatim, rated high; and (c) contemporary Jewish scholars (Rubenstein and Fonrobert). No encyclopedias, no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. Confidence: the open Sefaria Talmud (Shabbat 33b) and Pirkei Avot (4:13) are high (read line-by-line and quoted, carrying the historical figure and his teachings); Rubenstein and Fonrobert are medium (verified via publisher and journal records, not read line-by-line); Scholem is medium, carrying the same rating it holds in the zohar and kabbalah entries, and the de Leon attribution is recorded as an attributed scholarly position, not asserted as settled fact. Two minor attributions are flagged for honesty: the Pirkei Avot 4:13 saying names the speaker only as 'Rabbi Shimon' (the identification with Shimon bar Yochai is the standard scholarly one, not stated by the bare Mishnah line); and the veneration at Meron and the Lag ba-Omer hillula are a later, chiefly Safed-era kabbalistic custom, recorded here as attributed tradition, not as a fact about the historical Tanna. Relation targets: rabbi-akiva is a dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure (Rabbi Akiva); talmud, mishnah, rabbinic-judaism, zohar, kabbalah, and moses-de-leon exist (moses-de-leon authored in this batch).

lifespan.note: Second century CE; exact years are not established and the birth (c. 100) and death (c. 160) figures are only rough conventional markers for a Tanna of the generation after the Bar Kokhba revolt (died 135 CE), active in Roman Palestine. His traditional day of death is commemorated at Meron on Lag ba-Omer, but no reliably dated year is attested.

relations.note: The rabbinic tradition remembers Shimon bar Yochai as a leading disciple of Rabbi Akiva (c. 50-135 CE), one of the foremost Tannaim; much of Rashbi's transmission stands in the line of Akiva's teaching. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure (Rabbi Akiva).

As a Tanna of the second century CE whose teachings are recorded across the Mishnah, Tosefta, halakhic midrashim, and Talmud, Shimon bar Yochai is a central figure of the rabbinic movement. The rabbinic-judaism entry exists.

Many legal teachings are transmitted in the name of Shimon bar Yochai in the Mishnah (and the Tosefta and the tannaitic midrashim), reflecting his standing among the Tannaim whose traditions the Mishnah records. The mishnah entry exists.

The Talmud preserves teachings in his name and the famous narrative of his hiding in a cave from the Romans for thirteen years (Babylonian Talmud, Shabbat 33b), read for this entry on open Sefaria. The talmud entry exists.

In the traditional ascription the Zohar records the second-century mystical teachings of Shimon bar Yochai (Rashbi) and his circle, and its literary frame is set around him; this is the tradition's attributed position on authorship. Academic scholarship since Scholem instead attributes the bulk of the Zohar to Moses de Leon in late-thirteenth-century Castile. The authorship is disputed and is not adjudicated here, mirroring the zohar entry. The zohar entry exists and records the reciprocal relation.

Moses de Leon (c. 1240-1305) is the figure to whom modern academic scholarship attributes the bulk of the Zohar that the tradition ascribes to Rashbi; the two names stand at the centre of the Zohar-authorship dispute, which is not adjudicated here. The moses-de-leon entry is authored in this batch.

In the tradition's veneration of him and in the framing of the Zohar, Shimon bar Yochai is honoured as a great mystic and a fountainhead of Kabbalah; this reception is recorded as an attributed position, distinct from the historical Tanna of the rabbinic corpus. The kabbalah entry exists.

sources.citation: The Babylonian Talmud, tractate Shabbat 33b, open Hebrew/Aramaic and English text on Sefaria (William Davidson Talmud, CC-BY-NC). Cited as the openly-auditable primary text for the historical Tanna, read at the passage level: the narrative of Shimon bar Yochai's flight from Rome, in which he criticizes Roman public works ('everything that they established, they established only for their own purposes'), the sentence is passed ('Shimon, who denounced the government, shall be killed'), he and his son Rabbi Elazar hide in a cave where 'a miracle occurred and a carob tree was created for them as well as a spring of water', they study buried in sand for twelve years and a further twelve months, and emerge when the decree is abrogated at the emperor's death. Read line-by-line and verified against the text (Shabbat 33b).
sources.license: William Davidson Talmud (Koren-Steinsaltz), CC-BY-NC; underlying Aramaic in the public domain. Openly auditable; read at the passage level.

sources.citation: Mishnah, tractate Avot (Pirkei Avot) 4:13, open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria. Cited as an open primary text carrying a teaching transmitted in the name of Rabbi Shimon (bar Yochai), read at the passage level and quoted: 'There are three crowns: the crown of Torah, the crown of priesthood, and the crown of royalty, but the crown of a good name supersedes them all.' Confirms the transmission of teaching in his name within the tannaitic corpus. Read line-by-line and verified against the text (Pirkei Avot 4:13).
sources.license: Sefaria Community / open translation; underlying Hebrew in the public domain. Openly auditable; read at the passage level.

sources.citation: Rubenstein, Jeffrey L. Talmudic Stories: Narrative Art, Composition, and Culture. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 1999. A contemporary Jewish scholar's study of Babylonian Talmudic narrative; its chapter on 'Torah and the Mundane Life: The Education of R. Shimon bar Yohai (Shabbat 33b-34a)' treats the cave narrative as a rabbinic story shaped by its editors, comparing the Palestinian and Babylonian versions. Cited as the academic lens on the Shabbat 33b narrative. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Johns Hopkins University Press), not read line-by-line.

Fonrobert, Charlotte Elisheva. 'Plato in Rabbi Shimeon bar Yohai's Cave (B. Shabbat 33b-34a): The Talmudic Inversion of Plato's Politics of Philosophy.' AJS Review 31, no. 2 (2007): 277-296. Peer-reviewed article analysing the Shabbat 33b cave narrative of Shimon bar Yochai as a literary and cultural rabbinic composition; cited as an academic study of the same passage. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Cambridge Core, DOI 10.1017/S0364009407000529), not read line-by-line.

Scholem, Gershom. Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism. New York: Schocken Books, 1941 (based on lectures delivered 1938 at the Jewish Theological Seminary). Cited for the modern academic account of the Zohar's authorship, in particular the attribution of the bulk of the work to Moses de Leon in late-thirteenth-century Castile (c. 1280s), against the traditional ascription to Shimon bar Yochai; both are recorded here as attributed positions and neither is adjudicated. Verified to exist and be on-topic, not read line-by-line.
